senses.definition: to make someone feel extreme discomfort by helping them, being really generous, or by treating them too kindly, particularly someone who did one wrong

examples.x: When faced with a difficult customer, the experienced waitress chose to kill them with kindness, ensuring their dining experience was impeccable despite the initial complaints.

senses.definition: to be extremely angry with a person and be trying to find them and possibly hurt them
to use methods or tactics similar to those of one's opponent in a fight or argument

examples.x: When faced with a rival company's aggressive marketing campaign, our company had to fight fire with fire by launching an even more compelling advertising blitz.
The detective decided to fight fire with fire by using cunning tactics to outsmart the cunning criminal.
When addressing cyberattacks on their network, the cybersecurity team knew they had to fight fire with fire, employing advanced security measures to counter the sophisticated hackers.
She fights fire with fire, always ready with a sharp comeback when someone tries to criticize her.
He had fought fire with fire in the debate, matching his opponent's aggressive tone.

word_list.pronunciation: pˈeɪ ˌɛsbˈiː bˈæk ɪn wˈʌnz ˈoʊn kˈɔɪn ɔːɹ kɹˈaʊn

senses.definition: to retaliate by treating others in the same negative manner they treated one

examples.x: After enduring years of criticism from her coworker, Jane decided to pay her back in her own coin by providing honest feedback during a team meeting.
Mark felt that his neighbor was being excessively noisy late at night, so he decided to pay him back in his own coin by playing loud music in the morning.
When the rival company launched an aggressive advertising campaign against them, the business retaliated by using similar tactics to pay them back in their own coin.
Tired of his friend's constant teasing, Tom decided to pay him back in his own coin by poking fun at him in a lighthearted way.

senses.definition: a concept in which if someone harms one, one should harm them back in the same way

examples.x: In the courtroom, the lawyer argued that the defendant should receive an eye for an eye punishment, mirroring the harm they had caused.
Some people believe that responding to bullying with kindness is better than an eye for an eye approach.
The community leaders stressed the importance of forgiveness and reconciliation instead of seeking an eye for an eye after the conflict.
The manager's decision to reduce an employee's pay as retaliation felt like an eye for an eye response to their disagreement.
Many countries have moved away from the concept of an eye for an eye in their legal systems, opting for more humane and rehabilitative approaches to justice.

senses.definition: a hidden personal motive or agenda that someone holds

examples.x: Her sudden interest in my problems seemed suspicious, like she might have an ax to grind and was just trying to gather information.
The company's decision to donate to the charity raised eyebrows, with some suspecting they had an ax to grind and were seeking favorable publicity.
It's clear that there's an ax to grind in this political debate.
They've had an ongoing ax to grind since their school days.
There's an ax to grind between the two companies regarding a recent patent issue.
I could sense an ax to grind in her tone during the argument.

senses.definition: to angrily ask for a person or group to be punished or hurt

examples.x: The fans were baying for blood after their team lost the championship game.

word_list.pronunciation: bˈiːt ˌɛsbˈiː æt wˈʌnz ˈoʊn ɡˈeɪm

senses.definition: to outperform someone using the same methods and tactics that they use

examples.x: The small tech startup outmaneuvered the industry giant by launching a similar product but with faster innovation—truly beating them at their own game.
In the world of fashion, the new designer challenged the established brands by creating trendy designs that were in line with their signature styles, essentially beating them at their own game.
John decided to study his rival's sales techniques and ended up outselling him by applying those strategies, proving that he could beat him at his own game.

word_list.pronunciation: ɡɛt wˈʌnz ˈoʊn bˈæk ˌɑːn ˌɛsbˈiː

senses.definition: to seek to harm or punish someone who has wronged or harmed one

examples.x: After her coworker played a prank on her, she decided to get her own back by pulling an even bigger prank in return.
In the movie, the protagonist spends the entire plot trying to get his own back against the villain who wronged him in the past.
He patiently waited for the right moment to get his own back on the school bully who had been tormenting him for months.
Rather than seeking revenge, she chose to get her own back by excelling in her career and proving her worth to those who doubted her.
The team was determined to get their own back after losing to their rivals in the previous championship game.

word_list.pronunciation: ɡˈɪv æz ɡˈʊd æz ˌɛsbˈiː ɡˈɛt

senses.definition: to have enough strength and confidence to match others' ferocity and intensity during an argument or a fight

examples.x: When faced with unfair criticism, she gave as good as she got, calmly defending her point of view.
During the intense debate, both candidates gave as good as they got, exchanging strong arguments.
In the competitive business world, it's essential to give as good as you get to maintain your position.
Despite the harsh critique, the author gave as good as he got by responding with well-reasoned explanations for his work.

senses.definition: trying to punish, harm, or kill someone

examples.x: In the final minutes of the game, the opposing team was out for blood, relentlessly attacking our defense.
After the heated argument, it was clear that Sarah was out for blood and would stop at nothing to prove her point.
The corporate rivals entered the negotiation room, each one out for blood in the battle for the lucrative contract.
When the detective found evidence of foul play, he knew he was dealing with a suspect who was out for blood.
During the political debate, both candidates were out for blood, trying to discredit their opponent at every turn.

word_list.pronunciation: ɐ tˈeɪst dˈoʊs ʌv wˈʌnz ˈoʊn mˈɛdəsən

senses.definition: a harsh or unpleasant way of treating someone that is almost identical to the manner in which they treated one

examples.x: After years of lying to his friends, John got a taste of his own medicine when they discovered his deception.
The company's dishonest practices finally caught up with them, and they had a dose of their own medicine when their reputation suffered.
Sarah realized the importance of empathy when she found herself in a challenging situation, experiencing a taste of her own medicine from the past.
The politician, known for spreading false information about his opponents, received a dose of his own medicine when the media exposed his lies.
Tim had a taste of his own medicine when his colleagues ignored him during a critical project, mirroring his previous behavior of exclusion.

senses.definition: to hurt or punish someone in a way that ensures one they will never repeat their past behavior

examples.x: When the athlete violated team rules, the coach benched him to teach him a lesson about discipline.

word_list.pronunciation: tˈuː kæn ɔːɹ kʊd plˈeɪ ðæt ɔːɹ ðɪs ɔːɹ ðə ɡˈeɪm

senses.definition: used to show one's confidence in dealing with another person's behavior and using it to their disadvantage

examples.x: When Sarah realized her coworker was spreading rumors about her, she decided that two could play that game and started sharing some of her own about him.
After being constantly challenged by his rival, Mark finally decided that two could play this game and started competing even harder.
When Sarah realized her coworker was spreading rumors about her, she decided that two could play the game and started sharing her own positive achievements.

senses.definition: the action of taking revenge on someone for something unpleasant they did to one in order to get even or feel better

examples.x: When Sarah helped John with his project, he decided to offer tit for tat and assist her with her upcoming presentation.
In their ongoing feud, the neighbors engaged in a tit for tat exchange of complaints, each one reporting the other for minor infractions.
Instead of escalating the argument, Jane chose to respond with tit for tat, apologizing for her mistake after Mark admitted his own fault.
The two companies engaged in a tit for tat pricing war, lowering their prices in response to each other's discounts to attract customers.
Timmy and Tommy had a habit of tit for tat behavior, always sharing their toys equally to maintain a sense of fairness.

word_list.pronunciation: wˈaɪp ðə ðæt smˈaɪl ɡɹˈɪn smˈɜːk ˈɔf wˈʌnz fˈeɪs

senses.definition: to destroy someone's happiness or self-assurance through a remark, action, or event

examples.x: They've been laughing at their victim's expense, but the judge will wipe the smile off their faces.
When Susan found out about her surprise birthday party, her friends managed to wipe the smile off her face with their creative ruse.

senses.definition: used when a person attempts to embarrass or humiliate someone but gets embarrassed or humiliated instead
to decide to stop a particular activity or relationship

examples.x: After years of working together, they decided to call it quits and pursue their own careers.
We argued for hours, but in the end, we agreed to call it quits and put the disagreement behind us.
The company's financial losses were unsustainable, so they had to call it quits and close the business.
The players were exhausted, and the game was tied, so they called it quits and decided to resume the match another day.
Their marriage had been strained for years, and they finally decided to call it quits and get a divorce.

senses.definition: to make someone feel less important or influential by showing that their claims are not true

examples.x: When the new employee challenged the manager's decision, the manager put them in their place by explaining company policies.
She tried to dominate the discussion, but her colleagues quickly put her in her place by presenting stronger arguments.

senses.definition: to make things fair by addressing a previous unfairness or imbalance
